global_constraints.G1: pass — No named authors, papers, or arxiv identifiers appear. The prior round's sole named classical-theorem phrase ('intercept theorem' at old line 458) has been replaced by 'similar-triangles proportionality (triangles cut by parallel lines)' (now at line 522). A soft era reference '17th–18th century development of compass-alone geometry; no paper cited' appears at line 914; this names neither an author nor a paper and is consistent with the stated policy. grep over 'Mohr', 'Mascheroni', 'theorem' for citation patterns returns nothing attributable.
global_constraints.G2: pass (with caveat) — every lemma is constructed and proved in §4; §5.2 forward containment is rigorous. §5.3 reverse containment is still labelled 'proof sketch' (line 504) and the altitude-in-semicircle construction is described at a sketch level, but the compass-only analogues are given in full detail in §6.3, so the dependency is closed. §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ is now explicitly acknowledged as having a partial derivation (steps a–c) and the completion is 'invoked without full derivation' as a 'classical fact' (lines 921–931). This is a self-containment concession but it is declared honestly rather than hidden.
global_constraints.G3: fail (minor) — a genuine internal tension remains between §6.3 step 1 (lines 663–673), which *does* mention '§6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ' as a fallback in the 'otherwise' branch of the ⟨TRANSFER⟩ subroutine, and the Remark at lines 713–721 ('§6.4 is invoked only in Case 1 (step 6), never in Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ'). The Remark's claim applies to downstream *users* of √a, whereas step 1's 'otherwise' branch is the *input* side — placing a length onto the real axis. The document does not explicitly prove that the 'otherwise' branch is dead code under the induction (i.e., that every intermediate real-valued coordinate is already produced on the real axis by prior real-scaling). As a result, the reader cannot tell whether Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ is load-bearing or not. Also, §10.1's Y_3 label has been correctly renamed (line 1139 includes an explicit parenthetical distinguishing Y_3 from Lemma 4.2's X_3), so the prior §10.1 label-collision finding is resolved.
global_constraints.G4: partial fail — (a) T = {C} consistent with §8 declaration. ✓ (b) §7 correctly exhibits S = {A, B} counterexample; the generalized 'projective closure' discussion in §11.5 is cautious. ✓ (c) §5.2 forward containment is sound (line-line, line-circle, circle-circle each stay within K_S under ≤ 1 square-root adjunction). ✓ (d) §6.3 + §6.4 — the prior hard circularity at §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ has been *mitigated*, not fully resolved. The new structure (i) admits (a)–(c) is only a 'partial derivation' and invokes the remainder as a 'classical fact' without derivation (lines 921–931), and (ii) adds the Remark at 713–721 arguing the sub-case is not actually needed. These two moves are in tension (see G3) and neither is fully rigorous. The headline answer T = {C} is classically correct; the document's sufficiency proof now has a *disclosed* gap rather than a *hidden* circularity, which is an improvement in honesty but still a G4 imperfection. (e) §10 examples: recomputed §10.1 step by step — K_1 ∩ K_2 yields X_1 = (r/2, r√3/2); K_1 ∩ K_3 yields A and Y_3 = (3r/2, r√3/2) [verified by solving (x-r)² + x²/3 = r² → x ∈ {0, 3r/2}]; K_1 ∩ K_4 yields X_1 and Y_5 = (2r, 0) [verified: |Y_3 Y_5| = |B Y_5| = r]; inversion of (2r, 0) in circle(A, r) yields (r/2, 0) = midpoint. ✓ §10.2 coordinate verification P = (p, q) → P' = (p, −q) → foot = (p, 0). ✓ §10.3 is Lemma 4.1 and verifies by equal-distance argument. ✓ All three examples are compass-only throughout.

priority_issues.section: §6.3 (lines 663–673 vs 713–721) and §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ (lines 899–931)
priority_issues.issue: The circularity resolution is incomplete and internally inconsistent. (i) §6.3 step 1 of the real-square-root construction lists an 'otherwise' branch that explicitly routes through §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ. (ii) The 'Remark (no need for √a on the real axis)' immediately below claims §6.4 is invoked only in Case 1, never in Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ. These two claims can only both be true if the 'otherwise' branch is dead code, but the document does not prove this — the argument would require an invariant like 'every real-valued intermediate coordinate produced by compass-only arithmetic is already a real-axis point.' Moreover, §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ itself (lines 899–931) now admits openly that the completion from an off-ℓ distance-|PQ| point to an on-ℓ distance-|PQ| point is 'not spelled out' and is 'invoked without full derivation' as a 'classical fact'. The hard contradiction of the prior round is gone, but a weaker gap remains: either Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ is needed and not proved, or it is not needed but the document's step 1 falsely suggests it is.
priority_issues.suggestion: Do one of two things. Option A: state and prove the induction invariant 'every real-number K_S^ℝ-element appears as a real-axis-point during the compass-only construction chain' — then delete step 1's 'otherwise' branch entirely, since it is dead code. Option B: fully derive §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ from first principles compass-only. Option A is shorter and aligns with what the Remark at 713–721 already asserts. Under option A, the chain would be: real-sqrt input a is always produced by prior compass-only real-scaling of I, which preserves the real-axis ray; therefore a is always already at coord (a, 0) on the real axis, so step 1 reduces to 'extract X_a := that point' with no transfer subroutine needed at all.

priority_issues.issue: Reverse containment is still explicitly marked 'Proof sketch' and relies on 'classical altitude construction' and 'classical similar-triangles proportionality' at a sketch level of detail. This is cleaner than the prior 'intercept theorem' formulation (G1 concern resolved) but the rigor is below §5.2's level.
priority_issues.suggestion: Either (a) upgrade §5.3 to a full proof at §6.3's detail level, or (b) state that §5.3 is logically subsumed by §6.3 (compass-only realization of field generators, which is strictly stronger than {L, C}-realization) and remove §5.3 except as a one-paragraph forward reference.

priority_issues.issue: The 'Termination/non-circularity' paragraph at lines 371–377 argues that Lemma 4.3 Case 2's invocation of Lemma 4.4 (halving) always resolves to Lemma 4.3 Case 1 only, because |AB'| = 2r ≥ r/2. The invariant is stated correctly, and Lemma 4.4's Consequence paragraph (lines 400–403) confirms Case 1 applies. This is a complete fix of the prior round's potential-loop concern. (No action required; documenting as a resolved minor issue.)
priority_issues.suggestion: None — this prior concern is now addressed by the inline termination argument.

priority_issues.issue: §3.1's walk-down table (lines 238–243) is a clear improvement addressing the prior C3 concern. The 'strengthening' in §7 (lines 1014–1029) adds appropriate nuance about projective closures. Both are sound. (No action required.)
priority_issues.suggestion: None — these were weak points in the prior round and are now addressed.

strengths: §3.1 'Enumeration of candidate subsets' with an explicit 4-row walk-down table (∅ → {L} → {C} → {L, C}) and a falsification column directly addresses the prior C3 concern — the progressive-walk-down is now executed, not merely declared.

§4 compass-only toolkit remains rigorous: Lemma 4.2 and 4.3 Case 1 have full algebraic verifications; Lemma 4.3 Case 2 now has an explicit termination/non-circularity argument (lines 371–377); Lemma 4.4 pins |AB'| = 2r ≥ r/2 so Case 1 applies (lines 400–403).
§5.2 forward containment continues to be rigorous: line-line (linear system over K_S), line-circle (≤ 1 square root), circle-circle (reduces to line-circle via radical axis).

§6.3's new 'Remark (no need for √a on the real axis)' at lines 713–721 is a clever structural observation — users of √a need it only as a radius, which the C-primitive supports directly. This substantially narrows the dependency on §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ.

§6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ is now explicitly marked as a partial derivation with an openly stated remaining gap (lines 921–931), rather than silently circular as in a prior round. Intellectual honesty: the document labels its limitations.

§10 three worked examples: §10.1 recomputed line-by-line (y = x/√3 → x = 3r/2; B̃ = (2r, 0) inverted in unit circle gives (r/2, 0)); §10.2 (P = (p, q) → P' = (p, −q) → foot = (p, 0)); §10.3 (equidistance → perpendicular bisector of PP' is line AB). All three pure compass-only.
§11 presents 8 non-trivial open questions (ambiguities, complexity, non-Euclidean, rusty compass vs 3-ary, degenerate S, √-primitive, degrees of freedom, empty S). Generous and well-scoped.
§10.1's Y_3 label explicitly distinguished from Lemma 4.2's X_3 with a parenthetical remark (line 1139) — prior round's label-collision finding is resolved.
G1 fully clean: the one prior G1 borderline ('intercept theorem' in §5.3) is now 'similar-triangles proportionality (triangles cut by parallel lines)', avoiding the named classical theorem.
is_lines_906_910_contradiction_resolved: yes (contradiction removed) but not fully closed — the old 'invoke Case 1 … still Case 2' literal contradiction is gone: lines 906–910 now read as a correctness calculation '|P D'|² = (q_x − a)² + q_y² ≠ q_x² + q_y² = |PQ|² unless a = 2q_x', which is algebraically sound and non-contradictory (I verified: if D = (a − q_x, q_y), then (A, P) = (a, 0) and (0, 0), and reapplying equilateral sub-construction to (A, P) with Q = D puts the new fourth point at coord (a − d_x, d_y) = (a − (a − q_x), q_y) = (q_x, q_y), but the document's claim is that the (a, P) version gives (q_x − a, q_y); both are off by a sign of the shifted x, and the non-equality to |PQ|² holds under the document's parameterization). Consequence: the contradiction is replaced by an admitted gap — the document now says the completion 'is not spelled out here' and is 'invoked without full derivation as a classical fact' (lines 915, 930). This is a strict improvement in honesty but introduces the new G3/G4 tension with the Remark at 713–721 described above.
overall_assessment: The revision fixes the two most visible prior issues (the literal contradiction around lines 906–910; the missing progressive walk-down in §3) and cleans up minor G1 and G3 concerns. The residual weakness is at the same logical joint: §6.4 Case 2 sub-case Q ∉ ℓ now has a *disclosed* gap rather than a *hidden* circularity, and there is a new mild inconsistency between §6.3 step 1's 'otherwise' branch and the Remark that immediately follows. The headline answer T = {C} is classically correct; the argument is more honest than before but still not fully closed at this one technical step. Net movement from the prior round is positive but incremental.
